differences.title: 1. EXECUTION LOCATION
differences.leftDesc: Components execute entirely on the server, reducing client workload and improving initial load times.
differences.rightDesc: Components execute in the user's browser, enabling interactivity and dynamic updates after initial load.

differences.title: 2. INITIAL LOAD
differences.leftDesc: Only necessary HTML is sent initially, leading to quicker perceived page loads.
differences.rightDesc: Requires JavaScript to download and execute, potentially delaying content rendering.

differences.title: 3. CODE EXECUTION
differences.leftDesc: Can access server-side resources like databases directly without exposing credentials.
differences.rightDesc: Relies on JavaScript to run, making it susceptible to network conditions and client device capabilities.

differences.title: 4. INTERACTIVITY
differences.leftDesc: Interactivity handled by streaming UI or calling client components.
differences.rightDesc: Built for rich user interactions, state management, and dynamic UI updates.

differences.title: 5. DATA FETCHING
differences.leftDesc: Can fetch data directly from databases or other server resources securely.
differences.rightDesc: Data fetching typically done via API calls, requiring serialization and network latency.

differences.title: 6. BUNDLE SIZE
differences.leftDesc: Reduces the amount of JavaScript sent to the client, optimizing performance.
differences.rightDesc: Client components contribute to the JavaScript bundle size, impacting download and parse times.

differences.title: 7. STATE MANAGEMENT
differences.leftDesc: State primarily managed on the server or passed down as props.
differences.rightDesc: Relies on client-side state management solutions for dynamic user experiences.
